coredata.dc:title: Carbazole-naphthalene based colorant with rub induced and alkali sensitive fluorescence for security printing and latent fingerprint development
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Mehta, Riya
Jain, Rakshitha K
Sunil, Dhanya
P J, Anand
coredata.dc:description: New functional materials that exhibit fascinating optical features are highly valued due to their diverse applications in anticounterfeiting and forensic science. A novel carbazole-naphthalene Schiff base derivative, CNS is developed as a smart mechanofluorochromic pH-sensitive pigment for solvent based anti-counterfeiting ink. The screen prints on UV dull paper exhibit good substrate adherence and rub resistance. The inked region is weakly fluorescent under 370 nm UV light, but display intensified orange fluorescence upon rubbing, allowing easy print authentication. Different emission colors observed under various excitation wavelengths before and after rubbing, enhance the security elements. Further, the prints showcase NaOH triggered change in the fluorescence color. These covert features ensure document and product authenticity, offering robust data security and anti-counterfeiting measures. Further, the applicability of the ground CNS in forensic science is established to distinctly observe levels 1 to 3 details of latent fingerprints on non-porous and semi-porous surfaces.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbazole-naphthalene Schiff base
Rub-induced fluorochromism
Alkali-sensitive fluorescence
latent fingerprints

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) Emission spectra and b) chromaticity diagram of CNS in THF-water mixtures (1 × 10–4 M); λex = 370 nm, fw = 0–99 %. c) Optimized geometry, and molecular orbitals of CNS. d) Emission spectra and e) corresponding chromaticity diagram of CNS in THF-hexane mixtures (1 × 10–4 M); λex = 380 nm.
a) Emission spectra; λex = 370 nm and b) corresponding CIE diagram for CNS and CNS-g. c) Comparison of FTIR plots of CNS and CNS-g, d) 1H NMR spectra of CNS and e) PXRD plots of CNS and CNS-g.
Chromaticity diagram of a) CNS and b) CNS-g; λex = 250–430 nm. c) Emission spectra and d) CIE diagram of CNS subjected to varying pH (λex = 320 nm).
SEM images of a) blank and b) screen printed print proofs. c) CIELAB color space with colorimetric values of rub tested print proofs.

a) Photographs of screen-printed sample a) before and b) after rubbing. CIE plots of screen prints c) before and d) after rubbing under different excitation wavelengths (250–430 nm). e) CIE plot of screen print treated with NaOH; λex = 370 nm.

a) Non-porous, b) semi-porous and c) porous substrates: i) LFPs visualized under 365 nm UV illumination by staining with CNS-g, ii) Grey scale pixel profile, iii) 3D interactive plots of the selected marked region and iv) extraction of ridge features.
Colorimetric and densitometry values of printed and rub-tested printed samples.

head.ce:title: Carbazole-naphthalene based colorant with rub induced and alkali sensitive fluorescence for security printing and latent fingerprint development

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Manipal Institute of Technology, Manipal Academy of Higher Education, Manipal 576104, Karnataka, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aDepartment of Chemistry, Manipal Institute of Technology, Manipal Academy of Higher Education, Manipal 576104, Karnataka, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Manipal Technologies Limited, Manipal 576104, Karnataka, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bManipal Technologies Limited, Manipal 576104, Karnataka, India

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence author at. Department of Chemistry, Manipal Institute of Technology, Manipal Academy of Higher Education, Manipal-576104, Karnataka, India.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, Manipal Institute of Technology, Manipal Academy of Higher Education

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: New functional materials that exhibit fascinating optical features are highly valued due to their diverse applications in anticounterfeiting and forensic science. A novel carbazole-naphthalene Schiff base derivative, CNS is developed as a smart mechanofluorochromic pH-sensitive pigment for solvent based anti-counterfeiting ink. The screen prints on UV dull paper exhibit good substrate adherence and rub resistance. The inked region is weakly fluorescent under 370 nm UV light, but display intensified orange fluorescence upon rubbing, allowing easy print authentication. Different emission colors observed under various excitation wavelengths before and after rubbing, enhance the security elements. Further, the prints showcase NaOH triggered change in the fluorescence color. These covert features ensure document and product authenticity, offering robust data security and anti-counterfeiting measures. Further, the applicability of the ground CNS in forensic science is established to distinctly observe levels 1 to 3 details of latent fingerprints on non-porous and semi-porous surfaces.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The illicit production of counterfeit banknotes, legal documents and branded/trademark goods is a major worldwide issue that has caused a significant financial impact on the public, companies, governments, and consumers [1]. Further, forged pharmaceuticals and medications can pose severe health threats to society. Therefore, substantial research efforts have been made to create innovative materials and smart methods to identify genuine articles and to prevent illegal replicas of products/security documents/currency notes that create a large social impact [2,3]. The research in the anticounterfeiting solutions arena is mainly focussed on electronic, marking, chemical, physical, mechanical, and digital technologies [4,5]. Chemical and physical antiforgery solutions use markers to identify and verify products by utilizing the signals obtained when certain chemical/physical processes are applied on them. Marking materials include the use of thermochromic, hydrochromic, photochromic, fluorescent, and phosphorescent compounds as colorants in formulating anticounterfeiting inks with complex security and rapid authentication features [6–11]. Mechanical anticounterfeit solutions generally rely on the physical properties of materials to craft effective anti-tampering barriers [12]. When mechanical and chemical techniques are used alone, simple authentication is possible whereas, when they are used in combination, multi-level security can be achieved.

The safety of documents/branded products must be increased by employing high security anticounterfeiting materials and advanced authentication technology. New inks with superior security elements that are harder to duplicate are always in demand. Smart materials that own superior features that will be masked for the forger but could be easily authenticated by the user as colorants in security inks is of high significance. Optical anticounterfeiting applications can be derived from fluorescent molecular systems that exhibit distinct emission colors in response to varied stimuli including heat, humidity, solvents, pressure/force, UV/NIR illumination etc. [13,14]. The fluorescence behaviour of these stimuli-induced materials has garnered a lot of research attention, as they can be modified using a variety of techniques, such as bond formation and breakage, disrupting the intermolecular interactions, modifications/alterations in the packing arrangements and well-ordered assemblies of molecules, and reorganisations in molecular conformations [15]. Mechanofluorochromic materials that exhibit brilliant solid-state emission are often associated with aggregation-induced emission (AIE) and have garnered significant attention among stimuli responsive fluorophores [16,17]. However, developing these unique materials by overcoming fluorescence quenching due to their aggregation, and incorporating newer security elements into the same material can have immense applications as a marker pigment in security inks for information storage, smart packaging, bioimaging, security printing, and highlighting markers.

Molecules with acyl hydrazone linkages, belong to a class of photochromic molecules that are renowned for being dynamic and reversible in nature [18]. This bond is particularly favored in molecular design due to their straightforward synthesis, cost-effectiveness, stability, and unique structural properties. The acyl hydrazone bond is susceptible to external stimuli and can change its structure in response to changes in temperature, light exposure, and pH level; making these molecules serve as potent photo-tunable switches [19]. Acyl hydrazone units can generate multi-color switchable fluorescent emissions depending on their assembled states [20]. As fluorochromes, acyl hydrazones have an extended π-conjugated backbone that can cause a green-yellow emission due to a red shift in fluorescence [21]. These compounds are both nucleophilic and electrophilic; the carbonyl oxygen atom (C = O) is attacked electrophilically, while the amine nitrogen atom (NH) is attacked nucleophilically. Moreover, as the N CH bond is close to the amide nitrogen (CO–NH), the acyl hydrazone is slightly acidic, which facilitates the formation of intermolecular hydrogen bonds. Further, the conjugated structure of compounds based on acyl hydrazone linkages is primarily responsible for their luminous features, since it enables π-π* transitions following activation by UV or visible light. The introduction of electron-donating or electron-withdrawing groups to the hydrazide or carbonyl components allows for the modulation of the electronic environment around the hydrazone bond, effectively tuning the fluorescence emission wavelength and intensity. These imines are well recognized to exhibit mechanofluorochromic and AIE capabilities [19,20,22]. Hence, a carbazole-naphthalene Schiff base (CNS), which incorporates acyl hydrazone moiety was designed and synthesized as a colorant to fabricate security inks with the goal of showcasing stimuli-responsive fluorescence.

In forensic science, developing fluorescent latent fingerprints (LFPs) using powder dusting presents several challenges including poor visualisation of finger-marks due to rough surface texture, uneven adherence of the fluorescent powders leading to unclear prints, background fluorescence, high fluorophore cost etc. New fluorophores that can overcome these drawbacks and effectively develop LFPs on surfaces with complex backgrounds are highly sought after to enhance their detection across various substrates [23,24]. Therefore, the synthesized small molecule CNS is also explored for the development of LFPs on different surfaces. The present study is thus mainly centred on the synthesis of CNS and investigation of its structural, thermal, and optical features to be used as a suitable smart functional colorant in formulating a solvent-based security ink and in the development of LFPs. Instant prints obtained on UV-dull security paper with attractive optical and good rub-resistance properties can be used in diverse contexts as anticounterfeit solutions. The fluorescent fingermarks developed using CNS could detail level 1–3 features in nonporous and semi porous substrates.

Synthesis of N'-((9H-carbazol-3-yl)methylene)-1‑hydroxy-2-naphthohydrazide (CNS): A methanolic mixture of 3‑hydroxy-2-naphthol acid hydrazide (2 mmol) and 9-ethyl-3-carbazolecarboxaldehyde (2 mmol) was refluxed for 28 h. The precipitate formed was filtered, washed with methanol, dried, and recrystallised to obtain pure CNS (Scheme 1 ).

The melting point of CNS was checked using open capillary method. The molecular structure of CNS was confirmed by recording IR (Shimadzu – IR Spirit analyzer), NMR (400 MHz Bruker spectrometer) and mass spectra (Xevo QToF, Waters Mass Spectrometry System). The electronic (absorption and emission) spectra were collected using 1800 Shimadzu UV–visible spectrophotometer and JASCO spectrofluorometer FP 8300. Computational simulations were performed to gain deeper insights into the electronic and structural properties of CNS. These simulations used the B3LYP (Becke's three-parameter hybrid functional combined with the Lee-Yang-Parr correlation functional), a widely used density functional theory (DFT) method known for providing a good balance between accuracy and computational efficiency. The calculations were conducted in a vacuum environment using the 6–31G++ basis set, which includes both polarization and diffuse functions to better describe electron distribution, particularly in systems involving lone pairs, π-conjugation, or non-covalent interactions. All simulations were executed using Schrödinger's Materials Science Suite. The powder X-Ray Diffraction (PXRD) data was recorded from 5 to 80° (4°/minute) using Rigaku Miniflex 600 (5th gen) equipment.

The solvents, acrylic emulsions, and additives used for preparing the ink were obtained from Evonik Industries, Kamson's Pvt. Ltd, BASF - Chemicals, and DOW Chemical Company. The composition (in weight percentage) of solvent-based ink intended for screen printing involves CNS (15.8 %), screen medium (58.24 %), J003 retarder (11.21 %), and cyclohexanone (14.75 %). The particle size was determined using Hegman gauge by placing a small amount of the ink. The viscosity of the ink was checked at a shear rate of 0.1–1000 s-1 at room and elevated temperatures. The initial coating of the prepared ink using drawdown and later screen printing were done on UV dull paper. The thickness of the substrate (0.121 mm) and grammage (80 gsm) were measured with a digital GSM tester and a Mitutoyo thickness gauge. The paper substrate was coated with an ink layer using the K bar 0 number coater. The ink was drawn across a 120-mesh and pressed through the stencil's openings onto the substrate using a rubber-bladed squeegee for screen printing. Sutherland ink rub tester was used to check the adherence of the ink to the substrate by rubbing the printed paper against unprinted on a test rubber pad of 4 pounds. The colorimetric data was recorded using Xrite I1 Pro spectrophotometer. The CIELAB color coordinates including L*, a* and b* were determined. The ΔE, which provides the differences in color was calculated using the following equation;
Δ

)
2

Δ

)
2

Δ

)
2
where
(
Δ
L
*
)

L
*
( u )
−
L
*
,
(
Δ
a
*
)
=
a
*
( u )
−
a
*
and
(
Δ
b )
=
b
*
( u )
−
b
*
are the differences between the printed paper samples and (u) is the unprinted paper sample [25,26]. Carl Zeiss EVO 18 analytical scanning electron microscope (SEM) was used to examine the surface morphology and topological features of the paper samples.

Literature evidence suggests AIE and mechanofluorochromic properties of several Schiff bases [25,26]. Our previous report identified a fluorene-based Schiff base pigment FBH (Scheme 1) for document/product authentication [27]. However, the fluorescent colorant did not showcase any mechanofluorochromic properties. Further, we replaced the salicyloyl hydrazide unit with a bulkier 3‑hydroxy-2-naphthohydrazide group to synthesize FNH (fluorene and naphthalene-derived Schiff base) as depicted in Scheme 1, which exhibited both mechanofluorochromic and AIE features [28]. In the present work we designed yet another Schiff base CNS by replacing the fluorene unit of FNH with a N-ethyl substituted carbazole unit. The structural framework of CNS obtained through simple condensation reaction as depicted in Scheme 1 was confirmed using spectral techniques. The characteristic features of the molecule were further explored as presented in the subsequent sections for use as a smart functional pigment to prepare a solvent based screen-printing ink formulation. Carbazole is an organic fluorophore with a fused aromatic framework and a unique tricyclic configuration consisting of two benzene rings and a five-membered ring that contains nitrogen. Its structural arrangement bestows it special resonance, electrical, and photophysical properties. N-ethyl carbazole's high electron-donating capacity and extensive π-conjugation could significantly improve the fluorescence properties when integrated into a Schiff base structure as in CNS pigment and thereby that of the resulting ink.

The absorption spectrum of most organic compounds exhibits π–π* and/or n–π* transitions between 200 and 700 nm [28,29]. The CNS dissolved in THF (1 × 10–4 M) exhibited two distinct absorption bands at 290 nm and 350 nm due to its conjugated structural framework as shown in SF5. AIE molecular luminogens including FNH and FBH appear non-emissive in diluted liquids, but in aggregated form or in the solid state, emit fluorescence [30–33]. The same solutions exhibited emissions at 443 and 600 nm upon excitation at 370 nm. To investigate the aggregation tendency of CNS, the emission behaviours of CNS in THF-water mixtures, with the percentage of water (fw) as poor solvent varying from 0 - 99 % was monitored as presented in Fig. 1a . It is observed that solution with fw = 0 % displayed emission at 447 and 600 nm. Interestingly, a rise in fluorescence intensity with increasing water content was observed, which reaches its maximum at fw = 50 % and 60 %, indicating the formation of aggregates. The CIE plots (Fig. 1b) vividly depict the shift in the emissions with respect to the colour coordinates when the water content rises from 0 to 99 %. The photographs of the respective THF-water mixtures are presented in SF6.

DFT studies were performed to calculate the energy difference between the lowest unoccupied (LUMO) and highest occupied molecular orbitals (HOMO) to determine the chemical reactivity of CNS [34,35]. The optimised geometry, HOMO and LUMO of CNS generated using DFT simulations along with the respective electronic structures are portrayed in Fig. 1c. The energy values of HOMO and LUMO are −5.69 and −1.47 eV respectively with an optical energy gap of 4.22 eV. It is observed that in the LUMO the electron density is more confined towards naphthalene and the spacer moiety, whereas in the HOMO the electronic delocalisation is seen on the carbazole unit. These alterations in the electronic distribution are suggestive of intra molecular charge transfer (ICT) in CNS. To confirm this observation gained from the theoretical studies, emission features of the Schiff base in solvents of varying polarity (1 × 10–4 M THF-hexane mixtures of CNS) were examined. In Fig. 1d, a 10 nm hypsochromic shift is observed as the non-polar hexane fraction increases, confirming the ICT process occurring in the molecule. These spectral shifts as seen in Fig. 1e are expected to occur primarily due to the chemical interactions between the fluorophore's dipole moment and the reactive fields created in the surrounding solvent when the polarity of the solvent changes [36].

Interestingly, CNS powder exhibited a visible colour transformation from off-white to yellow (CNS-g) upon grinding with a mortar and pestle as shown in SF7a-b. For detailed investigation of the excitation and emission properties exhibited by CNS and CNS-g, the fluorescence spectra of CNS and CNS-g were obtained as depicted in SF7c-d and Fig. 2a to observe significant changes. For 370 nm excitation, both CNS and CNS-g showed common emission around 450 nm, but an additional emission peak is observed at 580 nm for CNS-g. Subsequently, the CIE plot (Fig. 2b) shows the colour coordinates of both the samples, which provides strong evidence for mechanofluorochromism exhibited by CNS.

To better comprehend the mechanism underlying stress induced emission behaviour, the FTIR spectra of both CNS and CNS-g were obtained. Except for the minor shifts in the C = N (from 1601 to 1622 cm-1) and NH stretching (from 3248 to 3255 cm-1), similar vibrational peaks are perceived for both CNS and CNS-g (Fig. 2c). This result confirms the retainment of the structural integrity of FNH even after exposure to mechanical stress. However, the peak shifts indicate the grinding induced probable disruption of Hydrogen-bonds and possible modifications in the molecular geometry and in turn packing arrangement. Hence, to gain deeper insights on the critical role of the functional groups in H-bonding, 1H NMR spectra of CNS (10 and 30 mM; DMSO‑d6) (Fig. 2d) were examined. The signal of the hydroxyl proton shifted from 12.01 to 11.973 ppm (0.037 ppm shift) with increase in concentration confirming the intermolecular H-bonding that exist between the carbonyl O and aromatic OH of two neighbouring molecules. Furthermore, the PXRD studies of CNS and CNS-g of equal masses were performed to examine the probable phase transformations upon grinding. The sharper reflection peaks in CNS (Fig. 2e) compared to broader peaks upon grinding as in CNS-g indicate more crystalline nature of the as-synthesized pristine sample. The reduction in the reflection intensity could be due to the destruction of the crystalline lattice [37,38]. The mechanical force applied on CNS could have induced a transformation from stable crystalline state as in CNS to a metastable semi-crystalline form as in CNS-g.

Interestingly, CNS and CNS-g exhibited different fluorescence colors upon excitation at varying wavelengths from 250–430 nm, as shown in Fig. 3a and 3b In the case of CNS, fluorescence color changed from purple to cyan, whereas CNS-g showed a different path in fluorescence color change from pink to yellow and finally to green with increasing excitation wavelengths. In addition, fluorophores that are responsive to pH stimuli generally offer significant advantages in security applications. Due to the prospective protonation-deprotonation processes possible in CNS, the pH triggered fluorescence responses of the Schiff base were probed after addition of H2SO4/NaOH [39]. As shown in SF8, the colourless solution turns yellow when pH rises to 8–10. A remarkable bathochromic shift was observed when the solutions become basic in nature as observed in the photographs in SF8 and emission spectra in Fig. 3c. The corresponding CIE plot (Fig. 3d) shows the emission colours of the solutions with varying pH. Further, as CNS is prepared for use as a functional pigment for security printing, the ability of the colorant to withstand the heat during the printing processes without any degradation was checked using TGA and the respective thermogram (SF9) shows that the pigment is sufficiently stable upto 300 °C.

The mechanofluorochromic and pH sensitive emissions of CNS upon UV light illumination are beneficial features that could offer covert security elements when printed on suitable substrates. Therefore, a solvent-based ink was prepared using the components as mentioned in the methodology section. The ink was off-white coloured (SF10) and the particle size of the CNS pigment in the formulation was found to be <10 µm. The mechanical properties of the ink were analyzed by examining the relationship between shear rate and viscosity. It was observed that the viscosity decreased with increasing temperature, exhibiting non-Newtonian fluid behavior, as shown in SF11. The inked area obtained after screen printing on UV dull paper was examined using SEM and compared with the surface topographical features of blank paper sample. As shown in the micro images (Fig. 4a and 4b), the surface texture of the blank paper was found to be fibrous, while the printed area displayed a comparatively smoother topography with the homogeneous ink film deposited over the fibrous and porous cellulosic paper.

The CIELAB color spaces that are intended to estimate human eyesight are used to define the colour of the printed area. The CIE values represent the color of the inked area as perceived by an average observer under a standard light source. The parameters x and y signify red:blue and green:blue ratios, respectively [40–42]. The chromaticity parameter L*, which has values between 0 (black) and 100 (white) closely resembles how the human eye observes color lightness. The values of a* and b* lie between −120 and 120: negative and positive a* values denote green and red, respectively, whereas negative and positive b* values indicate blue and yellow, correspondingly. The ΔE value suggests color difference; the print is invisible to human eye if the value is 0–2, experts can recognize if 2–3 and any individual can recognize if ΔE>3 [43]. The L* value (93.05) of the blank substrate is very close to 100, and the perceived color is white. As observed in Table 1 , though the printed paper sample displayed L* value close to unprinted paper sample, the greater positive b* value which is higher than the lower a* value indicates that yellow is the predominant hue of the ink layer [44,45].

Yet another important factor that determines the quality of the print is its resistance to rubbing, smearing, marking, or scuffing that might occur during various handling procedures, such as packaging, delivery, and usage [44]. Hence, the L*, a*, and b * values of the prints after 25, 50, 75, and 100 strokes were compared to assess the ability of the printed ink film on the paper surface to resist abrasion. The results showed that the L* and a* showed negligible changes upto 100 rubs. This suggests very little change in the brightness and red-green color, indicating that the print did not fade or shift much in these color directions. However, the b* value increased with more rubbing, suggesting the print became slightly more yellow. Additionally, the ΔE value (which indicates the overall color difference compared to the unrubbed print) also increased. This rise in ΔE suggests that the print is slightly becoming darker as the number of rubs increases. The L*, a*, b*, and ΔE values after the rub test compared to the unrubbed sample as listed in Table 1 provides evidence for the good rub resistance of the ink films on the paper. The graphical presentation of the colorimetric values of the paper sample after rub test is portrayed in Fig. 4c.

The screen-printed region was further investigated for various security features that could be utilized for authentication and identification of genuine prints on documents/branded products/pharmaceuticals. The print is weakly emissive under 365 nm UV illumination (Fig. 5a ). However, upon rubbing with hard objects an intense orange fluorescence is observed when viewed under 370 nm illumination as presented in Fig. 5b Thus, the forger observes only a faint fluorescence under the commonly used 365 nm light source, whereas the user can validate the originality of the document/product by rubbing the print with hard objects, to observe an orange emission under the same illumination source. Moreover, as the pristine CNS and ground CNS-g exhibited different fluorescence colour trails when illuminated with light sources of different wavelengths, we recorded the emission features of the unrubbed and rubbed printed areas. The print demonstrated emission colours moving from blue to cyan when the excitation wavelength was shifted from 250 to 430 nm as portrayed in Fig. 5c. As expected, the rubbed printed region showcased a totally different fluorescence colour trail from purple to yellow to green under the same excitation light sources (Fig. 5d). This fascinating emission features in terms of varying fluorescence colours for the unrubbed and rubbed printed areas when shined with UV and visible light sources bestows the product/document with higher-level covert security feature for checking its authenticity. Moreover, as CNS displayed pH induced fluorescence colour variations, the printed area was exposed to different pH. The printed sample exhibited human eye perceivable yellow fluorescence under 370 nm light source when treated with NaOH as presented in the inset of Fig. 5e. The corresponding CIE plot (Fig. 5e) validates the emission colour when the prints are exposed to different pH, which could be used as an additional authentication feature to check the genuineness of the documents/items.

Fingerprints remain a crucial tool for forensic experts in crime investigations [45]. LFPs were developed through powder dusting method using CNS-g and visualized on non-porous, semi-porous and porous substrates as shown in Fig. 6a -c. LFP matching utilizes unique features categorized into three levels. Level 1–3 features are visible in photographs of LFPs on non-porous (steel) and semi-porous (plastic) substrates when viewed under 365 nm UV light. On the porous (paper) substrate, the level 1 feature was clearly observed. The presence of CNS-g on the ridges is clearly demonstrated by the significantly higher grayscale values in these areas, whereas the furrow regions exhibit noticeably lower values. Thus CNS-g could be a promising fluorophore for LFP development, which provides a legal and dependable basis for individual identification in forensic applications.

The present comprehensive study demonstrates the easy synthesis of a new Schiff base, integrating carbazole and hydroxy naphthalene units. The new molecule CNS designed as a single functional colorant for fluorescent inks displayed multilevel security elements for document, passport, and product validation. The smart CNS pigment showcased various fascinating features such as ICT, AIE, mechanofluorochromism, and pH sensitive emission. The solvent-based CNS formulation successfully printed onto paper through screen-printing formed prints with good substrate adhesion. The prints using CNS ink displays i) rub induced fluorescence, ii) different emission colours for unrubbed and rubbed printed region under various excitation sources, and ii) NaOH induced fluorescence colour change. These multiple covert features including mechanofluorochromism, fluorescence color differences upon varying excitation wavelength, and pH-sensitivity, all incorporated in a single ink formulation could be conveniently used with promising applicability in advanced anticounterfeiting and data security. Further, the LFP images developed and visualized using CNS-g revealed 1–3 levels ridge details in non-porous and semi-porous substrates for personal identification in forensic application.

The data underlying this study are available in the published article and its Supporting Information. Supporting Information: Spectral traces of CNS and additional experimental details including photographs.
The authors declare no competing financial interest.

Riya Mehta: Writing – original draft, Validation, Investigation, Formal analysis. Rakshitha K Jain: Writing – original draft, Validation, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Dhanya Sunil: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Anand P J: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors are thankful to Manipal Institute of Technology, Manipal Academy of Higher Education for all necessary support.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.molstruc.2025.142279.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent advances in persistent luminescence based molecular hybrid materials

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Gao R., Kodaimati M.S., Yan D. Recent advances in persistent luminescence based molecular hybrid materials. Chem. Soc. Rev.. 2021;50(9):5564–89.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photoluminescent and chromic nanomaterials for anticounterfeiting technologies: recent advances and future challenges

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Abdollahi A., Roghani-Mamaqani H., Razavi B., Salami-Kalajahi M. Photoluminescent and chromic nanomaterials for anticounterfeiting technologies: recent advances and future challenges. ACS. Nano. 2020 Oct 20;14(11):14417–92.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Invisible luminescent inks and luminescent films based on lanthanides for anti-counterfeiting

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Li J., Xia D., Gao M., Jiang L., Zhao S., Li G. Invisible luminescent inks and luminescent films based on lanthanides for anti-counterfeiting. Inorganica Chim. Acta. 2021 Oct 1;526:120541.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Versatile design and synthesis of nano-barcodes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Shikha S., Salafi T., Cheng J., Zhang Y. Versatile design and synthesis of nano-barcodes. Chem. Soc. Rev.. 2017;46(22):7054–93.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: In situ synthesis of stimulus-responsive luminescent organic materials using a reactive inkjet printing approach

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Jeon S., Lee J.P., Kim J.M. In situ synthesis of stimulus-responsive luminescent organic materials using a reactive inkjet printing approach. J. Mater. Chem. C. 2015;3(12):2732–6.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Conversion of carbon dots from fluorescence to ultralong room-temperature phosphorescence by heating for security applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Jiang K., Wang Y., Cai C., Lin H. Conversion of carbon dots from fluorescence to ultralong room-temperature phosphorescence by heating for security applications. Adv. Mater. 2018 Jun;30(26):1800783.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Completely aqueous processable stimulus responsive organic room temperature phosphorescence materials with tunable afterglow color
